transcript.transcript: Hello, my name is Stephen Austin. I am a doctoral candidate in the Department of History at the University of Idaho. Today is Thursday, May 14th, 2025. I'm at the McClure center for Public Policy in Boise, Idaho, and today I'm speaking with former Idaho Congressman Larry Larocco to talk about his political career, focusing on wolf reintroduction in Idaho. The oral history. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of the American 2050 in Idaho commemoration. In addition, this information will be added in my ongoing dissertation research concerning the history of wolf reintroduction in Idaho. The Oral History Project, of which this interview is a part, supports the commemoration mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognition, appreciation and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Mr. Larocco, firstly, let me say thank you for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect and share your memories and experiences about wolf reintroduction, as well as your political career in Idaho. Through these questions, I hope to gain insight into the really existing culture of Idaho politics and learn more about your role in shaping wolf reintroduction. Mr. Larocco, thank you for taking the time. You bet. My pleasure to be here. Thank you for seeking me out. Well, thank you for responding. Uh, you know, my neighbor said, oh, you should have come to this, uh, this meeting because Larry Larocco was there speaking, and it was great. And so I've heard that you're making the rounds, uh, in the state of Idaho currently right now. And, uh, thank you for taking the time to sit down with me. Were you at the town hall in Moscow? I was, I was not, uh, we have a three month or a eight month old baby, so I don't leave the house very often. Yeah, but I heard it was great. It was a full house and it was, uh, well received. Uh, it was good to be back in Moscow. So were you. Uh, I know we'll get in a little bit to your, uh, biography, but you were the representative of North Idaho. I was, uh, the first district of Idaho runs from the Nevada border to the Canadian border. So it's the full, uh, breadth of Idaho north and south. It's over 500 miles long. Just out of curiosity, uh, at the time, how many constituents do you can you guess? How many were. Uh, about 700,000. And, uh, it was between 6 and 700,000. It was growing at the time, but now it's beyond that. Uh, and Idaho is waiting to get a third congressional district. They're just on the cusp of doing that, because now the congressional districts are almost a million, which is above average for the United States. Oh. Well, that's, uh, I'm sure that'll be interesting. Um, well, let's start with some some general biographic questions. Uh, just just to kind of warm up and then we can move on to the main subject. Um, but if you want to give a brief introduction about who you are and where you're, where you're from and whatever you would like to say. Well, um, I've had the great privilege of, uh, being one of about 12,000 Americans that have served in the Congress of the United States. And, uh, so my years of service in the US House of Representatives were 1991 to 1995. Um, so I had the privilege of serving the first district of Idaho, which is, uh, a wonderful district, uh, over 500 miles long, couple of time zones of the longest free flowing stretch of river in the United States, and just an amazing landscape and wonderful people. Um, so, um, my political mentor that got me started down that path was Senator Frank church. I had the honor of working for him for six years as his North Idaho field representative. I was based in Moscow from 1975 to 1981. I took a break during my service in the Senate as a staffer to help coordinate his campaign in Montana and Oregon for president in 1976. But as my political mentor, he helped me formulate positions on natural resources and other issues. Uh, so important to Idaho. Um, I'm married for 59 years, got two wonderful children, three great grandchildren. And, uh, I went to school at the University of Portland, got a master's degree at Boston University, did graduate work at Johns Hopkins University, at the School of Advanced International Studies and International Affairs. And, uh, uh, that master's degree was interrupted by the Vietnam War. more. And so I went on to serve as a military intelligence officer from 1969 to 1972, and I didn't complete that degree. I just came home to Idaho and, and, uh, found a place to raise the family and got involved in the community, and and here we are. That's wow. That's quite the career. So I thank you. Thank you for your service. And, uh, it sounds more than I've done. Well, you've done a lot, and I appreciate all you've done. I, I had the honor after Congress of serving as the president of the, uh, United States Association of Former Members of Congress. It's a group of about 600 of us, former House and Senate members who still believe in public service. And, uh, we run a great program called Congress to Campus, which sends out bipartisan teams of two former members to college campuses to talk about public service and the issues of the day. Boise State has made good use of that program. I think the University of Idaho has in the past as well, but that's kept me in touch with my colleagues and and, uh, I must say, I did have an itch to run again after I lost in 1994, and I ran for the lieutenant governor in 19, in 2006 and the US Senate in 2008. But I was unsuccessful. But, um, I gave it a try and, and, uh, so I found great satisfaction in the world of public affairs and, and, uh, uh, working with projects like yours, um, to, uh, at least keep my commitment that, uh, I don't believe that my, uh, oath of office expires and that I should, uh, share what I was privileged to, uh, know in the Congress, uh, to that public service with others. Well, may I ask you, uh, what influenced you to get involved in in community slash political work when you came back from Vietnam? Um. I was not in Vietnam. Uh, actually. Sorry. It's a sticking point with veterans because I did not serve in in country, and I served during the Vietnam War, but I my length of service was two years in Germany, uh, after some intense training and in military intelligence. But, um, I was on orders twice for Vietnam, but never, uh, uh, made the trip. Um, I think it goes back to sort of my Catholic upbringing, in a sense, and my concern about social justice and, and, uh, uh, my, uh, sort of thoughts about being a social worker and helping people. And I realized, uh, uh, during my education that I did not want a static career. I wanted to be in the middle of things, and I wanted my daily job to be fluid and transactional in a sense, and changing and in touch with the world at large. So politics was a natural draw for me. And, um, and I had an opportunity to, uh, work on Senator Churches 1974 campaign. And I had great admiration for him. And then I approached him about a possibility of a position on his staff, and he thought that I would be ideal as a field representative. And I jumped at the chance. Uh, working with people in the casework aspects of congressional affairs is like being a social worker on steroids and where you can really help people. And I just dove into it, uh, with my heart and soul. And so that kind of led me to politics. It wasn't so much the ego part of it. It was just helping people and solving problems. Well, I did want to ask you, uh, when I, you know, like everybody these days, I found most of your bio or easily through Wikipedia. Um, but I saw that you had, you know, been a field worker for, uh, for church and was curious what goes into that? You just described a little bit of it's like being a social worker on steroids, but are you shouldering the phone on a regular basis or you're out in the community listening to real problems? Well, you're doing both, really. If, uh, church told me that he did not want me sitting at the desk all the time, and so he wanted me out. Uh, I had the great benefit of representing him from the salmon River to the Canadian border. And if you just think about that landscape for a minute, you just are in awe about, uh, the natural resources and the difference in the economy and, uh, and so forth. So, um, I, uh, took his advice to heart, and I, uh, I roamed all over that territory and got to know wonderful people. And I think I was fairly in touch with the issues representing him and so forth. So, uh, if you keep your ears open and, uh, don't do all the talking and, uh, and are a good listener, you're going to hear of things. And of course, people write to Senator Church and then you, you help respond to those, uh, inquiries as well. So the aspect of casework certainly comes into it. But during that time, he was an active legislator on issues that, uh, involved, uh, that part of the state as well. Uh, most prominent was the River of No Return wilderness, uh, during that time. And what year was the I mean, I imagine there was a lead up into this. Well, there was by a noted, uh, environmentalists from the Idaho Primitive Area that had, uh, ideas on what, uh, Central Idaho Wilderness Bill would look like, which was more or less Drawn and conceived based on hydrology and and the protection of water and the resources in the area. So, um, and then putting it into legislative form, uh, became his job. Um, but I also worked on the Gospel Hump wilderness and the Saint Joe River, uh, designation and, um, and, uh, other issues, uh, you know, that, uh, were prominent in northern north Idaho at the time. So, so it sounds like, uh, you kind of had your political training on the ground speaking to you folks who had, you know, issues that they cared about that were directly affecting them. So how did that lead you to running for office? Well, uh, the first Congressional District has 19 counties. And, uh, when I worked for Senator Church, I represented him in ten out of 19 counties. And it was a very intimate, uh, kind of an affair. I mean it in a political sense. I got to know the people and the issues and the landscape very well. Keep in mind that at this time in 1980, there was one one Republican member of the legislature north of the salmon River, one. And he was a moderate Republican. Uh, now you can't find a Democrat in that territory. Uh, so that's how the political landscape has changed. So at that time, uh, my base was fairly, uh, you know, democratically oriented. Uh, it was very, uh, Lunchbucket kind of Democrat, uh, way of looking at things. But, um, so when he lost in 1980, I don't know if it was out of just emotional, uh, revenge or whatever. I decided I was going to run in 1982, which I did. I first ran for Congress in 1982, which was in the next election cycle after his loss. And, uh, so I wasn't, let's say, a civilian for too long that I teed it up and I spent 13 months running in 1982 for the for the first Congressional District against an incumbent at the time, Larry Craig, who had the seat. Okay. And, uh, were you were you successful? No I wasn't. Okay. Well, I was successful in that. I had a transformative year, and it was the most important year of my life. The only thing that wasn't successful is that I lost the election. Now, I got 46.5% of the vote against an incumbent Republican. Uh, which was not too shabby. Um, I raised a quarter of $1 million, and, um, I had, which I think was one of the most innovative campaign strategies in America. I took a job for a week at a time in every, uh, county in the congressional district. And at the end of eight months, I had 22 different jobs, uh, that I had, uh, taken and served in, uh, during that time. So, uh, it was innovative. I got a lot of national press and, and, uh, I got close. But in the end, uh, it just it didn't work. But in terms of personal growth and in touch with myself and climbing into the thin air of national politics, um, it was a dead stone winner. But, uh, I didn't get I didn't get a voting card. I didn't take the oath of office at that time. Well, so eventually you were successful, though. I was, so I'm just kind of curious, you know, the years between 1991 and 82, uh, what was it? Was it building more, uh, ground reputation or. Well, it was, um, uh, although there's the the realities of life. My kids were in the seventh and eighth grade when I ran in 1982. And, uh, they had every indication they wanted to go to college, and, and, uh, I should get a job. And so, you know, so the realities of life. As a matter of fact, Richard Stallings, who ran in 1982. He then ran. He lost in 82, but ran in 84 and won. And, uh, it was just financially impossible for me to tee it up again. So I became a stockbroker and, uh, after, uh, 82, um, although, um, I stayed involved and I ran for the state Senate in 1986 against gibberish. And it was in a large Floterial district in Ada County here. And I got 47% of the vote and the most expensive legislative race in the history of the state. And he was pro tem of the Senate. So I had taken on another incumbent and, uh, done well, but hadn't made it, uh, so, um, so there were some people who said, you know, Larocco just hasn't, uh, um, you know, close the deal. And so maybe, uh, he's not our guy, but I, I was relentless. And. And when the seat opened up in 1990, my wife and I decided that we would give it another shot. And the kids were in college and off we went, and we got through a three way primary. Um, and, uh, so that actually led, uh, the way to winning in 1990 because our campaign operation was in full gear and we hit the ground running after the May primary, and, and I defeated a five term state senator, uh, who had never lost an election. And I became the first Democrat in a quarter of a century to win. Very impressive. Well. That's my story. And it's a classic Idaho issue where it, it it, uh, you know, sort of confronts us our way of life with the federal presence and the federal lands. So we're it's not that we're not used to these types of issues. It just takes some getting used to. And and there's a lot of science involved. There's biology involved. But no kind of interaction with other, uh, Idaho politicians on this issue. Um, not really, not really. So, uh, what was your role? I mean, did you, I guess what was your observation after, uh, leaving Congress and you're back in the state, I take it. Yeah. Um, I stayed in Washington, actually. I worked for a banking group after that, and, um, but I, you know, you can't pull away from something that you've been part of for a long time. And, um, so I recognized that it was going to it was an issue that was going to need to get resolved. And probably, uh, there were there was going to be a lot of input from the ranchers because of the predation. And then there were various protocols put in place. And in terms of, uh, money that was paid, you know, I mean, if we're going to have reintroduction, then and the ranchers are going to say, well, yeah, well, what you know, I've got livestock out there and capital tied up and, and, uh, and you want me to graze on public lands, but yet if if I lose ten calves, uh, you know, so, so there had to be some accommodations and but science had to come into play at some point, too. I mean, the biologists had to come into, uh, onto the scene and inform people, um, and, and there were, you know, the ranchers took various, uh, positions on it. Somebody who influenced me was John Peavy, who was a rancher. And, um, he since, uh, he's recently died. But John is a conservationist and his operation is flattop. Ranch. And. Uh, um, uh, out of Carey, Idaho. And and he he helped school me on the ranchers perspective on on that type of thing. So so he had fish and game and you had to have accommodations between the Fish and Wildlife Service and the research they were doing, and fish and game and setting up bounties and, and setting up hunting, you know, uh, uh, uh, uh, seasons and so forth. And, and how you could trap how you could take them. Um, there was a issue, although during when I was in Congress on hunting, um, wolves from helicopters in Alaska. Really? Yes. And that came up and said, said this is the way that they were limiting the wolves on that is that they are hunting. And then, you know, then you had to come to a conclusion. Okay, well, so there's an intervention by man there in a way that is not us taking wolves by going into the field like we normally think of, but taking them from helicopters, which. So that seems like that's a way to reduce the population. And so if they're going to those extremes, then what what is this issue and what does that mean. And you have to figure out how many pups come out of a litter, right? I mean, and how fast that grows. And so how do you control the population? Well, we've normally control the population through hunting. Okay. Right now I do want to make one thing, uh, tell you one thing that I think it's still true. If you go into Congressman Simpson's office, Us. There's a stuffed wolf in his office, and. And he had this stuffed wolf because he was dealing with the issue on the front lines with, with, you know, after I left office. But I think he wanted people to see how big a wolf is and what it looks like in its prime. And just to show how big the paws are, you know, and what it looks like. It's not like it's, you know, a big dog or something. I mean, it's a large animal and it's. And it's beautiful. It's absolutely stunning. But, um, but that's a dramatic approach, and it sort of says, I'm in on this issue, you know? That's beautiful. Um, but it died and maybe it was shot and so forth, and I have it stuffed. And, um, so let's open the discussion, you know, and, um, I don't know many people. I don't think I've ever read a story about how Mike has that in his office, but it's it's it's a demonstrative display of, uh, an issue. Well, I guess I'm, you know, I'm grappling the way that I've. I've conceived of this dissertation in my mind is start from unfortunately, as most historians, you know, further back than the actual issue itself, uh, because I think there's antecedents to get to the discussion about Idaho's political character, political culture. Uh, but it sounds like during your time in politics, things, things changed in a way that I wasn't aware of. I had this idea that maybe it was, uh, maybe more, I don't know, 1970s, but this sounds like it was more in the 90s that there was a shift in, uh, voting patterns in Idaho towards something that's more similar to today, which is more conservative, and the wolves were a peripheral, peripheral issue. Um, when did they. It was. Was it during after your time that they or towards the end that they become this kind of hot button issue? Because I've heard proxy for other kind of values or cultural. Well, right. I mean here's what it is. It's, it's it's a confrontation with the federal government on Wolf. Introduction on on is are the feds going to tell the state what they can do? And so the warm up for this type of an issue was, uh, the spotted owl. Okay. And so that the spotted owl or the sage grouse or they're all kinds of issues like, like that where they're an endangered species. But then you have the whole concept of reintroduction. The word reintroduction. It's not introduction. It's reintroduction. Those two letters say a lot about bringing back to the landscape of Idaho what used to be there. So why do we have bison that are roaming around? I mean, you know, we could have wiped them out. Why did we bring back eagles and so forth? Why do we spend time on certain other species? Because so. But there's always been this feeling that the Endangered Species Act was driving out commodity groups and resource based industries and so forth to save something that was going to be extinct. So it's a statement about American values, and that it's a statement about federalism and the relationship between state and federal agencies and even the Frank Church Institute at BSU. We did a study at the Frank Church Institute about us up in five states, which was Utah, Montana, Uh, Wyoming. Um, Idaho and Nevada. And and one of the things that just pops out is this distrust of the federal government. So you see that. So I would say that your research would be enhanced by looking at that study just to do a snapshot of that. And, and, uh, Cheryl Österreicher, I think could probably help, uh, get you a copy of that because it just says almost the obvious, but it's there. The study was done. It was done by, uh, Morning Consult, and it was a good study. And so when you have this conflict, then it just sets up, uh, an issue pattern for people who want to cause problems or misinformation because there's always this already this distrust. And so if you're feeling like you're a victim out here in the west of the big bad federal government because your public lands state, then it's a no brainer for people to be promoting and exploiting those issues. Okay, so, um, I just never wanted to be exploited, and I and I don't have a demagogue streak in me, so I always sort of defaulted to, uh, science and biology and consensus. Right? I didn't think that was a bad way to proceed. And, uh, I mean, I didn't last more than four years in Congress, but that was my approach. And, uh, so. Well, I'm the the limited, you know, insight I have into Idaho's political history. It sounds almost, you know, for verbatim with other, uh, politicians who had come before you. And it was part of the consensus process, I believe it was Borah who was, you know, stayed over party, uh, trying to find some kind of way for this small state to be, uh, represented in, in, you know, find, uh, uh, reasonable governance. Well, don't forget, I mean, Jim McClure and Cassandras worked Together on a wilderness proposal. I mean, you know, McClure had a conservation ethic. And the way that he approached things and, and, uh, um, so that I think he had had a long history in, uh, legislative, uh, bodies and so forth. I mean, his whole life was dedicated to public service, and he sought consensus and and thought, well, maybe there's a way out of this instead of the heavy hand of the federal government just sort of airlifting wolves into areas. I mean, you know. Right. Uh, I've already mentioned, you know, you have the, uh, interaction with grizzly bears and people who think that, well, what they want to do is hike across the Selway-bitterroot wilderness. Well, you know, you better be prepared. There's a lot of stuff out there, you know, and, and, uh, uh, so, um, you know, we just try and live together on this planet and that's, that's part of it. Uh, um, but I think there are certain things that we don't want to compromise, and that's water quality. I mean, you know, we have the highest elevation spawning grounds for salmon and steelhead. Steelhead in the world. Did not know. That in the world. So why would we want to mess with that water quality, you know, when they're still going back to the spawning grounds in the in the Frank Church-river of No Return wilderness? I mean, so so we we've got to be mindful and have a conservation ethic as well. And so we'll see with with more people moving into Idaho. Will there be a growing conservation ethic or or, you know, will this movement to sell off public lands and make room for housing and and so forth? Uh, sort of dominate? I don't know. I that's for another generation. It's not for me, but, uh. Well, yeah, it's, uh, I guess a continuation of the same, same, uh, issues around public lands that have been there at least since the 70s. Yeah. Um, but I would ask you, you know, maybe more directly after after you got out of office, um, what did you have interaction with? You described a little bit of it, I think. But could you put a date to maybe interaction with other environmental issues? And did you did you do any lobbying or connection to the lobbying? I did in that regard was on, uh, for us geothermal, where I was, you know, doing some work on, uh, public lands, uh, um, regulations on the ability to, to, uh, um, uh, uh, you know, pull out, uh, geothermal water and so forth and, uh, for, for energy. Um, I was always on the periphery of, you know, just following events. And, uh, so from 1995, 11 years later, um, I ran for lieutenant governor and, and, uh, you know, a statewide race. But, um, you know, I was still attuned, I thought to things and, and keep in mind that the wilderness bill that I introduced, that once a wilderness bill like that is introduced in Congress, it sort of sets a template for what Congress thought about at one time should be included in the wilderness preservation system so that land managers on the ground will will always look at where those boundaries are. And so you do have an effect. So so by being active in these areas, um, it's helpful. For example, I introduced a bill to um, uh include the lower salmon River in the Wild and Scenic Rivers System as part of the salmon River system, so as a recreational river. So I would hope that the BLM managers would look at that bill and say that, you know, this was once considered for inclusion. And actually what I did is I adopted the to the BLM management plan and put it into legislative language and just said, let's just put the plan and just protect it. Under this rubric of of the Wild and Scenic Rivers System. It's not under there because my predecessor, my successors didn't take that that issue on. But I think that by taking on these issues, you you do set, you know, sort of a template for what politicians are willing to do, especially if you hold hearings on it and so forth. And, and, and, uh, and you get co-sponsors or you move it through one body and so forth. Um, the snake River Birds of Prey area, 482,000 acres. It's a national conservation area. That was my legislation. And so it's now part of the it's now protected. And it's the, you know, it's the highest concentration of, of, um, birds of prey in North America. And so it took me two, uh, you know, Sessions of Congress to two Congresses, actually, to get the bill passed. That's that's what happens. But you have to start somewhere. And so I'm very proud of that legislation. And, uh, the fact of what we've done, but it's a it's a multi-use area out there. And you talk about the historical aspects of what went on down there in that canyon. There are a lot of folks live down there for a long time, you know, and I'm. Less aware or I'm less familiar with that. Uh, I show my my ignorance again, uh, as not being a native Idahoan. Um, but I was going to ask, with the time that we have, would you like to talk more specifically about some of the things during your time, and maybe even after that you're particularly proud of? Uh, around? It doesn't necessarily have to be resource conservation issues, but, uh, I'm happy to continue on because, uh, legislation sounds innovative and. Well, you know, I, I had four years to do it. I think I hit the ground running. I had, uh, a wonderful staff. Uh, five of us had worked for Senator Church, and, um, I think we took his way of doing business, uh, and politics and a highly ethical and efficient way and transparent way into, uh, my office. And and it really helped me, uh, hit the ground running. Uh, there's a little story about how I wanted to be on the Interior committee when I went to Congress, and there were four seats available, and there were four senior people ahead of me that wanted, uh, those four openings on the Interior Committee. So, um, the speaker of the House at the time was Tom Foley from, uh, Washington. And, uh, Foley, uh, knew that I had a history of, uh, public lands issues and so forth. And I think he needed a neighbor, you know, to to help him because he had forestry issues and so forth. So he expanded the committee by one. And, uh, and that's what a good neighbor will do, especially when he's speaker of the House. So. So when I first went to the to the chamber, to the hearing room, there was just this little dorky desk set up down at the bottom. And it was like I was, you know, the intern or something. And and so then, you know, that was my first meeting of the committee. And then I came back three weeks later and and the the carpenters had, you know, smoothed it out and my seat looked like it had always been there and so forth. So they were just wonderful times like that, that, um, that, um, uh, I took on, uh, an issue, um, in the East Mojave, for example, uh, under the Clinton administration wanted to make the East Mojave a national park. And, uh, there had been hunting in there for generations, and you can't hunt in a national park. And so I propose that, uh, the East Mojave become a preserve. And, um, uh, it angered a lot of my conservationist friends and, uh, my committee chairman who, uh, beat me in subcommittee and full committee, uh, when I introduced that amendment. And then I took an amendment to the rules committee, got it in order, and took it to the House floor, and, and, uh, um, I won, uh, that vote against my committee chairman. And, uh, that was a very interesting time in my political career to take on George Miller for an issue that he was passionate about, because he was in sync with Dianne Feinstein at the time, who was, uh, running the California Desert Protection Act. So anyway, I, uh, I prevailed. Uh, the East Mojave is a very successful preserve right now, and it's not part of the National Park Service, but it's protected. And, uh, so I, I just decided I didn't know how long my tenure was going to be in the House, and I was going to be an active member. And, uh, I was I was a whip. I was on the speaker's advisory committee. I spoke often on the floor of the House. I loved being in the in the chair during debates, uh, because we were in the majority. And, uh, I came home over 30 times a year and, and, uh, I think I'm very proud of my, my forest health bill, though I think that that, uh, since that was 30 years ago. And if you think of the growth in the forest over a 30 year period, uh, the danger of cataclysmic fires is even greater now. And, uh, so it was, uh, it was an attempt to take the forest back to historical levels of density and so forth. And and my environmental friends were suspect. They didn't like the bill. But, uh, I think, uh, I think it it it should be resurrected. I once had an occasion to talk to Congressman Fulcher about it, but he never he never went into the archives and brought the bill out. Do you have, uh, any interaction with so at at the University of Idaho, they have, uh, what's called the Confluence Lab, and they're mostly environmental humanities folks, uh, English and whatnot. But their primary focus is fire. Um, and they do things similar to this where they're discussing, you know, stories and whatnot. They do awareness and so on and so forth. I don't know if they have any interaction with historic legislation or policy proposals, but have you ever discussed that? Because I have. And let me tell you what I think happens. I think people, uh, are different than you. You called me and I responded because I always respond. And thank you. But, um, a lot of people don't call because they think I'm not interested or I'm over the hill or I'd be a they'd be bothering me. And, um, um, I would, uh, speak to any high school, junior high, elementary graduate program class. Uh, when asked, I have never turned down an invitation to share what I've been lucky enough to learn through public service. And, um, um, and I've always felt that people who pick up the phone should be rewarded in some way, shape or form. But people don't pick up the phone. And, um, so I've been roaming around North Idaho since 1975 and, uh, um, and so I'd be happy to if, if you want to share that, um, uh, there's, there's history that's accessible in my archives on the forest health bill, which I think would be of interest to just throw on the table. Yeah, just to throw on the table because I held hearings on the bill and field hearings. Um, my subcommittee chairman, Bruce Vento, who was not a fan of the bill, actually came out and held the hearings because, um, he rewarded active members who believed in the process. Um, uh, some of these guys were from the class of 1974, and they were there for a purpose. And, uh, they believed in, in working and responding and so forth. So they, they loved active members. And I think I kind of fit in that category. And I think, uh, I got attention because of it, you know. Not just there necessarily to I mean, is that a common thing for freshmen congressmen to kind of play it safe or. Yeah, stay. Yeah. Yes it is. Okay. Yeah, but you passed several pieces of important legislation and proposed. Do you have a number off the top of your head? Maybe. How many? Well, um, the, uh, the Birds of Prey bill was one. There was a bill, uh, on a a coin, a coin bill that was a silver medal, uh, that I got signed into law. And then, uh, there was a bill to, um, in the Banking Committee to make, uh, uh, arrears on, um, uh, child support payments. Um, uh, that showed up on credit reports for deadbeat dads or deadbeat moms. So that was signed into law by Bush. And then there was, of course, the the biggest amendment was the, uh, the East Mojave, which I passed, you know, on and became part of an omnibus bill and then numerous amendments on water and so forth, and certainly in the Banking Committee I was active. So, um, so three standalone bills and then other things, um, I once sat in the steam room with a ten year veteran who was excited because he thought he was going to pass this first bill. So there's a different way of approaching, um, uh, Congress and the way that you serve. And, uh, we are in the majority. I took advantage of that. There are certain people that, uh, but I, I'm unaware of really anything meaningful that Fulcher has done, uh, on this. I, you know, it's just an approach, you know, it's just you kind of go along with stuff, and and, uh, I just didn't take that approach. I just loved the job. I thought. I thought it was the coolest job in America. Well, it's it's obvious that your, uh, your dedication to public service, uh, is real. And so, I guess, you know, with we have about ten minutes left. Um, I was, I was aiming to for these to be around an hour and a half. Um, but there's really no, no time limit. Um, but I guess if we're trying to aim towards the end. Um, can you speak a little bit about. You've already touched on some of these things about your time in politics under church coming up, uh, how they informed your ideas around consensus making around discussion. Um, can you speak a little bit about the importance of or, in your view, uh, public public, uh, you know, service and doesn't necessarily have to be at the congressional level? Yeah. I mean, um, I just finished Doris Kearns Goodwin's book on, uh, uh, about her husband, Dick Goodwin, one. And and his role in setting up the Alliance for progress and and the Peace Corps and so forth. And and so I grew up during a time when Kennedy was inspiring people to look at how we can help the world and use our educations and so forth, and broaden our reach out. And so, you know, I grew up at a time that I was impacted by that. And, um, um, and, you know, I, um, I was lucky enough to spend a year abroad when I was a sophomore, which was a transformative year for me. I spent a year in two semesters in Salzburg, Austria, and it sort of led me to do graduate work in international affairs and become fairly proficient in German. And but, you know, I saw, uh, Europe broadly, uh, 20 years after the Second World War and so forth, but my wife and I just both believed that she's an educator. And and I had this idea that, um, we could live an interesting life and help people at the same time and raise our kids and and, uh, I just don't see that inspiration anymore. And it kind of bothers me that that we're not being inspired. Uh, the reading, the the the current book. It almost brought me to tears to think back at these times when people, after he gave his speech at the University of Michigan, said, I'm going to do that, I'm going to go do that, and I'm going to help. And so, um, and think how enriching it was and broadening it was, it didn't create a bunch of woke people. It created a bunch of public servants who believed in public service and for all the right reasons. And and so that's how I I've kind of been proud of, you know, the Obama and the Clinton administrations, even Biden, you know, who. And, you know, when you look at the people who are in the administration, is there. not there as billionaires or whatever. So I become a little disillusioned about what's happening. And so the reason I got so active in the Congress to campus program is because at its core, it was a discussion of public service. That's all it was. And to say how we serve the public in a bipartisan way, because there was always a Democrat or a Republican speaking to the students. So you had that. And so I've spoken to students, you know, in junior colleges, and I've spoken to midshipmen at the academy, you know, and everything in between. And, uh, just to tell my story and to see if anybody's interested in that, what the path is, because public service is not always putting your name on the line. You can, um, I've met some of the most committed people who work for the Department of State, the Department of Commerce, or at DMV, for goodness sakes. You know, people who just do a good job and who believe in what they're doing. So, uh, and, you know, a lot of these jobs are secure jobs for people, and that that's turned a lot of people against them because, um, they have pensions and annuities. When we're in a world where, you know, financial illiteracy is rampant, but we're all sort of turned loose to fund our own retirement systems and so forth. Is that I think there's a huge disconnect. And, uh, right now where we don't like people who are in public service because they've got it easy. And so now we have Doge and, you know, and uh, um, uh, you know, and again, we get back to our original premise that we're interfacing here with the federal government in Idaho in a big way. So it just happens to be on a public lands way. And and also with the AFL and other things, mountain Home Air Force Base. I mean, we have sort of a large presence and in some ways. But, uh, so, um, um, it's been a good ride. I mean, I'm almost 79 years old and. Really. Wow. And, uh, I'm I'm excited about. Doing. Great. The world and and, uh, you know, we got problems. But, um, you know, today, my job is to finish up a column I write for the Lewiston Morning Tribune. I don't know if you've ever seen my column in the Lewiston Tribune. I have not. I'm sorry. That's okay. That's okay. A lot of people have. So three, three small kids keeps you kind of. Yeah. I get blind to the world. Right. Uh, so, I mean, I've tried to stay involved, and, uh, the 11 town hall meetings, and former Congressman Stallings and I have recently done. We just sent out, um, our report on our 11 town hall meetings, uh, which is an open letter to the current Idaho congressional delegation telling them what they've missed by not interacting with their with her constituents and being afraid of of meeting with them about what's going on in Washington, D.C.. So, um, I know it's going to be, uh, written in the statesman and, uh, on Friday and we'll see if it gets picked up. But it's an open letter to them. But that's been exhilarating. Uh, traveled the state and met with people and snowstorms and, and, um, you know, uh, junior high auditoriums and, uh, community libraries and. Yeah, people are afraid and they're fearful and and it all gets down to what we cherish. And that's our democracy and due process, rule of law and the Constitution. So those aren't bad things to protect. Not at. All. So. Well, former Congressman Larry Larocco, I really appreciate the time. Thank you for meeting me here at the McClure center. And, uh, hopefully we can speak again in the future. Great. You can call me anytime. I've enjoyed our time together. Thanks for asking. Thanks. Thanks for.

lines.line: > The speaker introduces himself as Stephen Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an interview with former Idaho Congressman Larry Larocco. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on wolf reintroduction in Idaho and its historical context. The purpose of the interview is to gather insights for Austin's dissertation and to contribute to a broader commemoration project.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces himself as Stephen Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an interview with former Idaho Congressman Larry Larocco. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on wolf reintroduction in Idaho and its historical context. The purpose of the interview is to gather insights for Austin's dissertation and to contribute to a broader commemoration project.

lines.line: > The discussion shifts to the controversial topic of wolf reintroduction in Idaho. Larocco provides insights into the complexities of the issue, including public and political reactions, the involvement of federal and state authorities, and the ecological and social implications. He emphasizes the importance of science and balanced policy-making in addressing the challenges associated with managing wolf populations in Idaho's wilderness.

metadata.title: Wolf Reintroduction in Idaho
metadata.synopsis: The discussion shifts to the controversial topic of wolf reintroduction in Idaho. Larocco provides insights into the complexities of the issue, including public and political reactions, the involvement of federal and state authorities, and the ecological and social implications. He emphasizes the importance of science and balanced policy-making in addressing the challenges associated with managing wolf populations in Idaho's wilderness.

lines.line: > Larry Larocco discusses his early political career, highlighting his service in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1991 to 1995. He mentions his mentor, Senator Frank Church, who influenced his views on natural resources and other significant issues in Idaho. Larocco's background includes extensive education and military service, which shaped his approach to politics and public service.

metadata.title: Larry Larocco's Early Political Career
metadata.synopsis: Larry Larocco discusses his early political career, highlighting his service in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1991 to 1995. He mentions his mentor, Senator Frank Church, who influenced his views on natural resources and other significant issues in Idaho. Larocco's background includes extensive education and military service, which shaped his approach to politics and public service.

lines.line: > The speaker reflects on his initial unsuccessful congressional campaigns and the lessons learned from them. Despite early setbacks, including a loss in his first congressional race, Larocco's persistence in politics eventually led to his election. He discusses the dynamics of his campaigns, the changing political landscape in Idaho, and the strategies he employed to connect with voters across the state.

metadata.title: Challenges in Congressional Campaigns
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on his initial unsuccessful congressional campaigns and the lessons learned from them. Despite early setbacks, including a loss in his first congressional race, Larocco's persistence in politics eventually led to his election. He discusses the dynamics of his campaigns, the changing political landscape in Idaho, and the strategies he employed to connect with voters across the state.

lines.line: > Larry Larocco clarifies his military service and discusses his post-congressional career, including his role as president of the United States Association of Former Members of Congress. He expresses a strong belief in the value of public service and community engagement, reflecting on his ongoing efforts to educate and inspire others about the importance of civic involvement and the responsibilities of public officials.

metadata.title: Post-Congressional Career and Public Service
metadata.synopsis: Larry Larocco clarifies his military service and discusses his post-congressional career, including his role as president of the United States Association of Former Members of Congress. He expresses a strong belief in the value of public service and community engagement, reflecting on his ongoing efforts to educate and inspire others about the importance of civic involvement and the responsibilities of public officials.

story.title: Larry LaRocco- Wolf Reintroduction May 14, 2025
story.description: In this interview, former Idaho Congressman Larry Larocco reflects on his political career, focusing on the complexities of wolf reintroduction in Idaho and broader conservation issues. Larocco discusses his motivations for entering politics, inspired by a commitment to social justice and public service. The dialogue touches on the contentious nature of reintroducing wolves, highlighting concerns from ranchers, hunters, and conservationists. Larocco emphasizes the importance of consensus-building and trust in addressing such issues, rooted in a conservation ethic shared by Idahoans. Throughout, he reflects on legislative achievements and the continued importance of public engagement and service.
